name: Kanuri
description: The Kanuri are the descendants of the Kanem-Bornu Empire, one of the longest-lived empires in African history (9th–19th century CE). At its height, Bornu controlled the central Saharan trade routes, accumulating immense wealth from the trans-Saharan gold, salt, and slave trades. Kanuri identity is intertwined with Islamic scholarship, court culture, and the Mais (kings) of Bornu whose lineage continued for over a thousand years. Their heritage is now centred in Borno State, north-eastern Nigeria.
cultural_highlights: Kanem-Bornu Empire — one of Africa's longest-lasting empires; the 9th-19th century CE state controlled trans-Saharan trade
Islamic court culture — Arabic learning, Quranic schools, and sophisticated royal ceremonial traditions spanning 1000+ years
Mai (king) institution — the constitutional monarchy of Bornu, whose current lineage traces back over 1,000 years
Geidam salt trade — Kanuri merchants controlled ancient salt trade routes from Lake Chad to the Mediterranean

food_traditions.name: Biriyani (Borno variant)
food_traditions.description: Fragrant spiced rice with meat — a Saharan trade-route influence blending Arab, Hausa, and local flavours

food_traditions.name: Kilishi
food_traditions.description: Spiced dried meat jerky unique to northern Nigeria; Kanuri and Hausa traders spread this preservation technique across the Sahel

food_traditions.name: Lake Chad fish (Nile perch, tilapia)
food_traditions.description: Fresh and dried fish from Lake Chad remain staples of Kanuri cuisine, traded across the region

music_traditions: Goje (court fiddle) — used in royal panegyric music to praise the Mai and noble lineages
Kakaki (royal trumpet) — shared with the Hausa court tradition, played at state occasions
Dalilo praise singing — professional praise singers who recite lineage histories and court epics
